feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: The European Union's regulatory landscape in the area of digital services has become more complex in recent years. This rapid growth has led to a challenging regulatory environment, where it is not always clear how certain legislative instruments interact with each other. These include important legal instruments such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), the Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act) and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD).

We therefore call for priority to be given to the effective and efficient implementation of existing legislative instruments rather than the introduction of overlapping regulation. When issuing new regulations, we ask the European Commission to ensure consistency and consistency with existing legislation.

In addition, we call on the European Commission to respect the division of competences between the Union and the Member States, as laid down in the Treaty on European Union (TEU) and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU), when developing new legislative initiatives in this area.

New rules should only be developed within the limits of the competences conferred on the Union and in strict compliance with the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality (Article 5 (1) to (4) TEU). This applies in particular to the possible fixing of a digital age of majority, which was said by the European Commission's Thomas Regnier in June: an EU-wide ban "is not what the European Commission is doing. It's not where we are heading to. What? Because this is the prerogative of our member states."

An effective age control system is an important aspect of the online safety of minors. At the same time, we ask the European Commission not to impose an additional layer of age verification requirements on platforms already regulated under the DSA.

We support the risk-based approach adopted by the EU in the Guidelines on Article 28 of the DSA, as well as the assumption that different services present different risks and also require different forms of age control.
